ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03315091 matches 'curry puff' as a filled deep-fried pastry snack; sardine filling variant is not separately coded — exact match at the class level, filling variant undifferentiated.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pastry products — filled pastry / savoury pastry snack
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers filled savoury pastry products; no Singapore-specific curry puff code exists. Used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for curry puff or sardine-filled pastry snack as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard sardine curry puff piece weighs approximately 65 g, which is a typical single-serve portion sold at Singaporean hawker stalls and mamak shops.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Puff (Sardine) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11090011 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB entry. Saturated fat estimated at 7.5 g/100g based on HPB total fat 18.21 g/100g and typical deep-fried pastry saturated fat fraction (~41%) consistent with HPB compositional data for similar fried pastry snacks.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wheat-Based Pastry and Filled Savoury Snacks — analogous estimate based on refined wheat pastry products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for sardine curry puff exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 68 is estimated from the closest analogous food: refined wheat-flour deep-fried pastry snacks (GI range 65–72, medium category) as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. The sardine and potato filling contributes a moderate glycaemic dampening effect relative to a plain pastry shell. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 18.82) / 100 ≈ 12.8, where carbohydrate per 65 g serving = 28.96 × 0.65 = 18.82 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 391 mg/100 g combined with saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g from deep-fried pastry
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sardine curry puff to one piece occasionally, as the 391 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 254 mg per 65 g piece) combined with high saturated fat from deep-frying contributes to elevated blood pressure and arterial stiffness; choose baked versions and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat pastry shell driving medium-high GI (68) and glycemic load of 12.8 per serving with only 2.11 g/100 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume sardine curry puff sparingly; the refined wheat pastry shell produces a medium GI of 68 and a glycemic load of approximately 12.8 per 65 g piece, and the low dietary fibre content (2.11 g/100 g) provides insufficient buffering of the postprandial glucose response — limit to one piece and pair with a high-fibre vegetable side.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.5 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g from deep-fried pastry shortening, partially offset by sardine omega-3 content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high saturated fat (7.5 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g) from the deep-fried pastry shortening raise LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk, substantially negating the anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit of the sardine filling; a baked preparation would significantly reduce this risk and is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g and AGE generation from deep-frying negate sardine omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit; chilli is a nightshade ingredient
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried sardine curry puff because trans fat from pastry shortening and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated during deep-frying directly counteract the anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids in sardines; a baked version without shortening would be significantly more RA-appropriate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sardine curry puff (karipap sardin) is a deep-fried savoury pastry snack popular in Singapore, Malaysia, and among the mamak community. The outer shell is made from a short-crust wheat flour pastry that is crimped at the edges and deep-fried in oil until golden and flaky. The filling consists of canned or fresh sardine pieces, diced potato cubes, hard-boiled egg, onion, and a spiced curry paste mixture that typically includes turmeric, chilli, cumin, and coriander. The result is a compact, hand-held snack with a crispy, oily exterior and a savoury, mildly spicy filling with a soft, starchy interior. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 315 kcal, 8.98 g protein, 18.21 g fat (of which approximately 7.5 g is saturated), 28.96 g carbohydrate, 2.11 g dietary fibre, and 391 mg sodium. The sardine filling contributes omega-3 fatty acids and protein, but the deep-fried pastry shell dominates the saturated fat, trans fat, and refined carbohydrate profile. The glycemic index is estimated at 68 (medium), producing a glycemic load of approximately 12.8 per 65 g serving. Clinically, this food is of concern for hypertension (high sodium and saturated fat), Type 2 Diabetes (medium-high GI, low fibre), and cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat from deep-frying). It is a culturally significant street food and hawker snack in Singapore, commonly sold at mamak stalls, pasar malam night markets, and kopitiam coffee shops. Halal-certified versions are widely available. Allergens include gluten (wheat pastry), fish (sardine), and egg.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sardine curry puff?
How much sodium in a curry puff?
Is sardine curry puff good for heart health?
What is the GI of curry puff?
Is curry puff suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry F11090011 with review_status reviewed and confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from HPB total fat using defensible fraction for deep-fried pastry. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous refined wheat pastry snack category. All mandatory multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), full micronutrient panel (potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins A/B1/B2/B3/B6/B12/C/D/E, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for this specific sardine curry puff preparation are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F11090011 as of 2026; the HPB Foodo entry for this food item reports only energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains an exact match for sardine curry puff with a complete micronutrient panel. trans_fat_g set to 0.2 g/100g: estimated from typical deep-fried pastry shortening composition consistent with HPB compositional data for similar fried pastry snacks; not explicitly reported in the HPB source. saturated_fat_g of 7.5 g/100g is estimated from total fat 18.21 g/100g using a ~41% saturated fraction typical of deep-fried wheat pastry products in the HPB database. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database refined wheat pastry snack category; no direct GI measurement for sardine curry puff exists. Glycemic load per serving of 12.8 is calculated for a 65 g standard serving (GL = 68 × 18.82 / 100). The input record listed GL as 19.7 on a per-100g basis, which is consistent (GL per 100g = 68 × 28.96 / 100 = 19.7); the per-serving GL of 12.8 is the clinically relevant figure. Halal status assessed as true based on typical mamak preparation with no pork or alcohol ingredients; individual vendor certification should be verified. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible anchor for filled savoury pastry; no Singapore-specific curry puff FoodEx2 code exists.
